wine_history.name: Roman Lugdunum and early Gallic vines, 1st century BC to 2nd century AD
wine_history.description: The Romans founded Lugdunum (Lyon) in 43 BC as the capital of Gaul, and viticulture spread north along the Saone valley from Lugdunum into the granite and limestone hills that would become Beaujolais. Ancient sources and archaeological finds attest to vine cultivation in the region under Roman provincial administration.
wine_history.evidence: The Beaujolais wine board and regional histories document Roman-era vine cultivation spreading north from Lugdunum along the Saone valley.

wine_history.name: Benedictine and Cistercian monasteries shape the vineyard, 10th to 13th centuries
wine_history.description: Monastic orders, particularly the Benedictines of Cluny Abbey (founded 910) and later the Cistercians, held extensive vineyard plots across what is now the Beaujolais. The monks cleared hillside land, defined parcel boundaries, and kept records that documented site-by-site variation long before any appellation system existed.
wine_history.evidence: The Cluny Abbey foundation in 910 is well documented; regional Beaujolais histories record monastic land holdings in the Beaujolais hills during the medieval period.

wine_history.name: Lords of Beaujeu and the Vicomte era, 11th to 14th centuries
wine_history.description: The Seigneurs de Beaujeu ruled the territory from their capital at Beaujeu and promoted vine cultivation as both an economic and social marker. The town of Beaujeu gave the region its name and remained the Beaujolais capital until Villefranche-sur-Saone grew in commercial importance in the 16th century.
wine_history.evidence: The name Beaujolais derives from the medieval lords of Beaujeu; regional and municipal records document the seigneurial era and the town's role as the historic capital.

wine_history.name: Philip the Bold bans Gamay from Burgundy, 1395
wine_history.description: Duke Philip the Bold of Burgundy issued a 1395 edict calling Gamay a very bad and disloyal plant and ordering it expelled from the Cote d'Or in favour of Pinot Noir. The ban pushed Gamay south into the Beaujolais, where it found its true home on granite soils, and locked Pinot Noir into Burgundy proper. This single political act defined both regions for the next six centuries.
wine_history.evidence: The 1395 ducal edict is documented in the Bourgogne wine board history, the Burgundy wine reference, and multiple academic wine histories.

wine_history.name: The Lyon silk trade and the rise of the negociant, 17th to 18th centuries
wine_history.description: Lyon's growing textile industry created a permanent market for inexpensive Beaujolais table wine among the city's canuts (silk weavers), who famously drank from a pot lyonnais, the distinctive 46 cl bottle still used today. The Lyon negociant houses that emerged in this period handled the trade between the scattered small growers of the Beaujolais and the city's restaurants and taverns.
wine_history.evidence: The pot lyonnais bottle and the canuts' consumption of Beaujolais is documented in regional food and wine histories; the negociant role is covered in Beaujolais wine board records.

wine_history.name: First Beaujolais wine train to Lyon, 1860
wine_history.description: The arrival of the railway connecting the Beaujolais villages to Lyon around 1860 transformed the region's commercial structure. Barrels that had previously been carried down the Saone by boat or road now reached the city rapidly, enabling freshness-dependent primeur wines to be sold soon after harvest. The train accelerated the culture of early-release Beaujolais in Lyon's bouchons.
wine_history.evidence: The railway connection and its role in Beaujolais wine commerce is documented in regional transport and wine histories.

wine_history.name: Phylloxera devastation and replanting on grafted rootstock, 1870s to 1890s
wine_history.description: The phylloxera louse arrived in the Beaujolais in the early 1870s and destroyed a substantial proportion of the vineyards over the following two decades. Replanting on American grafted rootstock in the 1880s and 1890s reshaped the region; granite-soil plots on the hillsides were selectively retained while some valley-floor land was abandoned. The crisis ultimately concentrated Gamay on its best terroirs.
wine_history.evidence: Phylloxera's arrival and impact across the Beaujolais is documented in INAO regional histories and French viticulture records.

wine_history.name: First AOC grants for Beaujolais Crus, 1936 to 1937
wine_history.description: The INAO granted AOC status to Moulin-a-Vent (1936), Fleurie (1936), Morgon (1936), Chiroubles (1936), Brouilly and Cote de Brouilly (1938) in the foundational round of French appellation legislation. These Beaujolais Crus were among the earliest AOCs in France, recognising site-specific quality that growers had observed for generations.
wine_history.evidence: INAO official records document the 1936-1938 AOC grants for the Beaujolais Crus; the Beaujolais wine board cites these dates.

wine_history.name: Beaujolais Nouveau commercial release tradition begins, 1951
wine_history.description: French law in 1951 authorised the formal release of primeur wines and the Beaujolais negociant trade, led by Lyon merchants, seized the opportunity to sell young Gamay within weeks of harvest. The practice formalised a centuries-old tradition of drinking the new wine in Lyon's bouchons, giving it a legal and commercial structure that would eventually go global.
wine_history.evidence: The 1951 French legislation governing primeur releases is documented in INAO records and multiple Beaujolais wine histories.

wine_history.name: Jules Chauvet and the philosophical roots of natural wine, 1950s to 1980s
wine_history.description: Jules Chauvet, a Beaujolais negociant and trained biochemist based in La Chapelle-de-Guinchay, spent decades studying fermentation without sulphur dioxide and argued that terroir expression was masked by industrial additions. His work influenced a generation of young Morgon growers and laid the philosophical foundation for what became the natural-wine movement.
wine_history.evidence: Jules Chauvet's role as the intellectual forerunner of the Beaujolais natural-wine movement is documented in wine journalism and in the published accounts of the Gang of Four producers he mentored.

wine_history.name: Third Thursday of November fixed as global Beaujolais Nouveau release, 1985
wine_history.description: French authorities fixed the release date of Beaujolais Nouveau to the third Thursday of November in 1985, replacing the earlier and inconsistent mid-November arrangements. This single regulation, combined with the marketing apparatus built around it, turned what had been a regional Lyon custom into a global annual event. Midnight unlocking ceremonies spread from Tokyo to New York under the banner Le Beaujolais Nouveau est arrive.
wine_history.evidence: The 1985 fixing of the third Thursday of November as the Nouveau release date is documented in INAO records and universally cited in Beaujolais wine literature.

wine_history.name: Régnié promoted to Cru status, the 10th and newest Cru, 1988
wine_history.description: Régnié received its AOC Cru Beaujolais designation in 1988, becoming the tenth and most recent of the Beaujolais named Crus. The elevation recognised granite-soil Gamay from the villages of Régnié-Durette and Lantignié and completed the current roster of ten Beaujolais Crus that spans from Brouilly in the south to Saint-Amour in the north.
wine_history.evidence: The 1988 INAO grant of AOC status to Régnié is documented in INAO records and the Beaujolais wine board's appellation page.

wine_history.name: The Gang of Four forms in Morgon, 1980s
wine_history.description: Marcel Lapierre, Jean Foillard, Guy Breton, and Jean-Paul Thévenet, all Morgon vignerons influenced by Jules Chauvet, began vinifying without sulphur, chaptalisation, or filtration in the 1980s. Their collective approach became known as the Gang of Four and their wines, particularly Lapierre Morgon, became the founding texts of the international natural-wine movement, exported to the United States through Kermit Lynch Wine Merchant.
wine_history.evidence: The Gang of Four formation and their practices are documented extensively in wine journalism including Decanter, Wine Advocate, and Alice Feiring's writing on natural wine.

wine_history.name: Lapierre exported to the United States through Kermit Lynch, 1990s
wine_history.description: Importer Kermit Lynch Wine Merchant began bringing Marcel Lapierre Morgon and the other Gang of Four producers to the American market in the early 1990s, introducing Beaujolais natural wine to a new audience that had previously associated Beaujolais only with Nouveau. The Lapierre wines, especially the non-sulphite cuvee, became cult items in New York, San Francisco, and Chicago wine circles.
wine_history.evidence: Kermit Lynch Wine Merchant's importation of Lapierre and the Beaujolais natural-wine producers is documented on the importer's own site and in US wine press coverage from the period.

wine_history.name: Marcel Lapierre dies; sons Mathieu and Camille continue, 2010
wine_history.description: Marcel Lapierre, the figure most closely identified with Beaujolais natural wine and the Gang of Four, died in October 2010 at the age of 60. His sons Mathieu and Camille Lapierre took over the Domaine Lapierre in Villié-Morgon and continued both the winemaking approach and the Demeter biodynamic certification that Marcel had pursued in his final years. The domaine's reputation and export presence have grown continuously since.
wine_history.evidence: Marcel Lapierre's death in 2010 and the continuation by his children are documented in wine press accounts and on Domaine Lapierre's own site.

wine_history.name: The Beaujolais renaissance and critical reassessment, 2010s
wine_history.description: After decades in which the Nouveau phenomenon overshadowed the Crus, the 2010s saw a sustained critical reassessment of Beaujolais. Decanter, Bettane et Desseauve, Vinous, and Wine Advocate published in-depth Cru reports that positioned Morgon, Moulin-a-Vent, and Fleurie alongside minor Burgundy appellations in quality. Restaurant sommeliers in Paris, London, and New York drove demand for small-grower and natural Beaujolais.
wine_history.evidence: The Beaujolais critical reassessment is documented in Decanter feature articles, Wine Advocate coverage from the early 2010s, and the Beaujolais wine board's international marketing reports.

wine_history.name: Beaujolais natural wine reaches Brooklyn, Tokyo, and London, 2000s to 2010s
wine_history.description: The international natural-wine movement that the Gang of Four pioneered spread through the 2000s and 2010s to wine bars in Brooklyn, Tokyo, London, and Copenhagen. Beaujolais producers, particularly from Morgon and Fleurie, appeared on wine-by-the-glass lists across cities far from France and introducing a new generation of drinkers to Gamay and carbonic maceration.
wine_history.evidence: The global spread of Beaujolais natural wine is documented in trade press, Alice Feiring's writing, and export statistics published by Inter Beaujolais.

wine_history.name: Climate-driven structural rise in alcohol and evolving viticulture, 2010s to 2020s
wine_history.description: Rising summer temperatures across the Beaujolais through the 2010s and 2020s pushed Gamay to earlier harvest dates and higher sugar levels, producing wines with typical ABVs climbing from 12.5 to 13.5 percent or above in warm vintages. Forward-thinking producers responded by returning to older, higher-altitude plots, increasing canopy cover, and in some cases delaying destemming to extend hang time in cooler microclimate pockets.
wine_history.evidence: Climate effects on Beaujolais viticulture are documented in Decanter vintage reports, Inter Beaujolais technical publications, and producer accounts.

wine_history.name: The Beaujolais Premier Cru project, 2020s
wine_history.description: Inter Beaujolais, the regional trade body, launched a campaign in the early 2020s to seek INAO recognition of specific named lieux-dits within the ten Crus as Premier Cru sites, aiming to formalise the quality hierarchy that growers and critics had long recognised informally. Sites under discussion include Cote du Py in Morgon and Le Moulin-a-Vent in Moulin-a-Vent. The project was ongoing as of 2026.
wine_history.evidence: The Inter Beaujolais Premier Cru project is documented on the Beaujolais wine board site and in Decanter and Wine Spectator news coverage from 2021 onward.

wine_history.name: Wave of organic and biodynamic conversions across the Crus, 2000s to 2020s
wine_history.description: Following the lead of the Gang of Four, a broader wave of Beaujolais estates converted to certified organic and biodynamic viticulture through the 2000s and 2020s. Christian Ducroux (Demeter certified in Régnié), Domaine Lapierre (biodynamic-practicing), and multiple Morgon and Fleurie growers pursued formal certification. By the mid-2020s the Beaujolais had one of the highest proportions of organically farmed vineyards of any French appellation region.
wine_history.evidence: The organic and biodynamic conversion wave in Beaujolais is documented by Demeter France, Ecocert, and the Beaujolais wine board's sustainability reports.
